title: Staffing Firm Placement Capacity Score
subtitle: Find the bottleneck between your team size and your placement volume

persona_selector.text: Which best describes your staffing firm today?

answers.label: Boutique firm, 10-20 employees, mostly contingent placements
Growing firm, 21-40 employees, mix of contingent and retained
Established firm, 41-75 employees, multi-vertical operations
Owner-operator scaling into a real ops team

questions.text: How fast does a new job order move from first client contact to a recruiter having it in hand?

answers.label: More than 24 hours, often gets lost in email threads
Same day if someone remembers to forward it
Within 4 hours during business hours
Within 1 hour via a documented handoff
Under 15 minutes, automated routing to the right recruiter

questions.text: How are job order details captured from the client?

answers.label: Verbal on a call, written up later from memory
Free-form email or Slack from the account manager
A shared document template most people use
Structured intake form with required fields
Intake form plus AI-assisted enrichment and ATS sync

questions.text: How do you qualify whether a job order is worth working?

answers.label: We take everything that comes in
Gut call by whoever picks it up
Informal criteria the senior team knows
Scored against written qualification criteria
Automated scoring with fee, fillability, and client tier

questions.text: Can leadership see open job orders and their stage at any moment?

answers.label: Only if we ask each recruiter for an update
In a spreadsheet that is usually a week stale
In the ATS but recruiters forget to update stages
Real-time dashboard, updated daily by the team
Live dashboard with automated stage tracking and alerts

low.headline: Your intake is a leaky funnel
low.text: Job orders are entering through too many doors and details get lost between the account manager and the recruiter. You are almost certainly losing placements to firms that responded faster with cleaner information.
steps: Create one structured intake form every client request must pass through
Assign a single owner to triage and route new orders within 1 hour
Track every order in a shared pipeline view with stage and owner

mid.headline: Intake works, but it does not scale
mid.text: You have a rhythm that mostly works, but it depends on memory and individual discipline. When volume spikes, the cracks show up as missed orders or recruiters working stale briefs.
steps: Standardize qualification criteria with a written scoring rubric
Automate routing from intake form to ATS with the right recruiter tagged
Set a real-time dashboard leadership checks each morning

high.headline: Intake is a real operating system
high.text: You are capturing, qualifying, and routing orders in a way most firms twice your size cannot match. Your next lever is squeezing more signal out of the data you already collect.
steps: Layer AI enrichment on every new order to pre-score fillability
Run a monthly review of intake-to-placement conversion by client tier
Feed intake data into forecasting so leadership sees capacity 30 days out

questions.text: When a new role opens, how often do you already have qualified candidates ready?

answers.label: Almost never, we start from scratch every time
Occasionally, if we recently worked a similar role
About half the time we pull from prior searches
Most of the time we have a shortlist within a day
Nearly always, warm pools are maintained by vertical

questions.text: Where does candidate data actually live?

answers.label: Spread across inboxes, LinkedIn, and recruiter notes
Mostly in the ATS but with big gaps and duplicates
ATS is the system of record, data quality is uneven
ATS is clean, enriched, and searchable by skill and status
Unified candidate graph across ATS, LinkedIn, and outreach tools

questions.text: How do you re-engage past candidates who were not placed?

answers.label: We do not, we just source fresh every time
Only when a recruiter remembers a specific person
Quarterly blast email to silver-medalists
Segmented nurture sequences by role and availability
Automated reactivation the moment a matching role opens

questions.text: From job order to first qualified submittal, what is your typical time?

answers.label: More than 5 business days
3 to 5 business days
2 to 3 business days
Within 1 business day
Same day, under 4 hours for priority roles

low.headline: You are sourcing every role from zero
low.text: Your recruiters are spending most of their day hunting instead of closing. Every new job order forces them to rebuild a pipeline that should already exist, which directly caps how many placements any one person can make in a month.
steps: Audit your ATS for the top 10 roles you recruit and tag every qualified past candidate
Build a weekly cadence to enrich and status-check candidates in your top verticals
Create a silver-medalist list per vertical and assign an owner to nurture it

mid.headline: Your pipeline is a half-used asset
mid.text: You have candidate data, but it is not consistently feeding new searches. Recruiters still default to fresh sourcing because trusting the database is a gamble.
steps: Clean and deduplicate the ATS on a monthly schedule with clear ownership
Stand up automated reactivation sequences triggered by new job orders
Track time-to-first-submittal as a KPI every recruiter sees weekly

high.headline: Your pipeline is a competitive moat
high.text: Most firms your size cannot pull a shortlist on day one. You can. The leverage now is making sure this advantage shows up as higher fees and faster closes, not just faster submittals.
steps: Productize your speed by offering guaranteed shortlist SLAs to top clients
Use AI matching to rank your existing pool against every new order in minutes
Review quarterly which verticals have the deepest pools and double down

questions.text: How is a candidate matched against a job order?

answers.label: Recruiter reads resumes and makes a call
Keyword search in the ATS plus recruiter judgment
Boolean search plus a shared match checklist
Scored matching against a written job scorecard
AI-assisted ranking against a structured scorecard

questions.text: How long does it take to qualify a single candidate against a role?

answers.label: 15 minutes or more per candidate
Around 10 minutes per candidate
5 to 8 minutes per candidate
Under 5 minutes using a defined checklist
Under 2 minutes with AI-assisted pre-screen

questions.text: If two recruiters screened the same candidate, would they reach the same decision?

answers.label: Rarely, it depends heavily on the recruiter
Sometimes, senior recruiters are more aligned
Usually on clear fits, split on borderline cases
Yes, thanks to a shared scorecard
Yes, scorecard plus calibration reviews every week

questions.text: What percent of your submittals convert to client interviews?

answers.label: Under 20 percent
20 to 35 percent
35 to 50 percent
50 to 70 percent
Over 70 percent

low.headline: Matching is eating your recruiters alive
low.text: When qualification takes 10 to 15 minutes per candidate, a single recruiter can burn 4 hours a day just deciding who to talk to. That is time they should be spending on relationships and closes.
steps: Build a one-page job scorecard for every role before sourcing begins
Give every recruiter a 5-point screening checklist they must apply
Track submittal-to-interview rate by recruiter to spot calibration gaps

mid.headline: Matching is structured but slow
mid.text: You have discipline, but your qualification process is still mostly manual reading and judgment. The next 2x of productivity is in compressing the time per candidate without losing quality.
steps: Replace keyword search with structured scorecard ranking in your ATS
Pilot AI pre-screening on one vertical and measure submittal quality
Run weekly calibration reviews on borderline candidates across the team

high.headline: Matching is a speed advantage
high.text: You are qualifying faster and more consistently than most firms, and your submittal-to-interview rate reflects it. The opportunity now is to use that speed to win premium engagements.
steps: Package your speed as a retained or container offer at a higher fee
Feed interview and placement outcomes back into scorecard calibration monthly
Expand AI-assisted matching to every vertical once KPIs are proven

questions.text: How quickly does a candidate hear back after applying or being contacted?

answers.label: Days, and often not at all
Within 48 hours most of the time
Within 24 hours
Within a few hours during business hours
Instant acknowledgment with a clear next step

questions.text: How do clients get status updates on their open roles?

answers.label: Only when they chase us
Ad hoc emails from the account manager
Weekly recap email per client
Scheduled update cadence with shared pipeline view
Live client portal plus proactive alerts on stage changes

questions.text: How reliable is candidate follow-up after submittal?

answers.label: Candidates often go dark because we forget to check in
Recruiter remembers when they can
Calendar reminders trigger most follow-ups
Automated reminders plus recruiter personal touch
Full sequence from submittal to offer with zero dropped handoffs

low.headline: You are losing candidates and clients to silence
low.text: Every hour a candidate waits is an hour a competitor can reach them first. Every week a client does not hear from you is a week they question whether you are working the role at all. Silence is the cheapest way to lose revenue.
steps: Set a written response SLA for candidates and clients and post it team-wide
Automate first-touch acknowledgment on every application and inquiry
Commit to one scheduled client update per open role every week

mid.headline: Communication is consistent but manual
mid.text: You are responding and updating, but it costs your team meaningful time every day. Automation can take the baseline off their plate so they spend their time on the conversations that actually close deals.
steps: Automate candidate status messages at each ATS stage change
Stand up a weekly client recap template driven by pipeline data
Track response time as a KPI and review outliers in weekly standup

high.headline: Communication is part of your brand
high.text: Candidates and clients know what to expect from you, and that predictability is a reason they keep coming back. The next move is turning that reliability into pricing power and referrals.
steps: Turn client update cadence into a branded portal experience
Ask every placed candidate for a referral inside a structured workflow
Publish your response SLAs publicly and use them in new business pitches

questions.text: How clean is the handoff from placement to onboarding, timesheets, and billing?

answers.label: Manual and error-prone, we rebuild info each time
A checklist exists but breaks when volume spikes
Documented process, mostly followed
Systemized with shared tools across teams
Automated flow from placement confirmation to invoice

questions.text: How many hours per week do recruiters spend on admin and data entry?

answers.label: More than 15 hours per recruiter
10 to 15 hours per recruiter
6 to 10 hours per recruiter
3 to 6 hours per recruiter
Under 3 hours per recruiter

questions.text: How easily can you see placements, margin, and recruiter performance?

answers.label: Only by exporting spreadsheets and pivoting them manually
A monthly report that takes a day to build
Dashboards exist but lag by a week
Live dashboards reviewed weekly with the team
Real-time dashboards plus automated anomaly alerts

questions.text: If placement volume doubled next quarter, what breaks first?

answers.label: Everything, we would drown
Back office and billing, recruiters would be overwhelmed
A few specific bottlenecks we already know about
Minor friction but we have capacity headroom
We have already stress-tested the system for 2x volume

low.headline: Your back office is the ceiling on your growth
low.text: Your recruiters are doing three jobs, your admin is manual, and every spike in volume costs you margin and sanity. You will hire your way out of this problem at twice the cost of systemizing it.
steps: Map every back-office handoff from placement to invoice and find the top 3 break points
Automate timesheet and invoice flows with your existing ATS and accounting tools
Cap recruiter admin time at 5 hours per week and redesign workflows to fit

mid.headline: Operations work, but they will not scale
mid.text: You have process, but too much of it lives in people's heads. Doubling placements would force you to hire aggressively instead of letting your systems absorb the volume.
steps: Systemize your top 5 recurring workflows with automation, not more hires
Build a live dashboard for placements, margin, and recruiter KPIs
Run a quarterly capacity review to pre-empt the next bottleneck

high.headline: Your operations are a growth engine
high.text: You could add meaningful volume without proportional headcount, which is rare in staffing. The opportunity is to get more aggressive about growth because your ops can absorb it.
steps: Pressure-test the system at 2x current volume with a tabletop simulation
Reinvest recovered recruiter hours into business development and retention
Productize your ops as a differentiator in client pitches and renewals

cta.headline: Want help turning your weakest category into your biggest unlock?
cta.description: Book a 30-minute strategy call and we will map the highest-leverage automation and AI moves for your firm, based on your score and your actual workflows. No pitch deck, just a working plan you can run with whether we work together or not.
